name: Aqua Appia
description: First aqueduct of Rome, built during the Samnite Wars. Ran almost entirely underground from springs east of Rome.

name: Anio Vetus
description: Second aqueduct of Rome, drawing water from the Anio river. Financed by spoils from the war against Pyrrhus.

name: Aqua Marcia
description: Longest of the eleven Roman aqueducts, famous for the purity and coldness of its water. First to use arcaded bridges extensively.

name: Aqua Tepula
builder: Cn. Servilius Caepio & L. Cassius Longinus
description: Named for its lukewarm (tepid) water, drawn from volcanic springs in the Alban Hills. Later merged with Aqua Julia on the same arcade.

name: Aqua Julia
description: Built by Agrippa, carried on the same arcade as the Tepula and Marcia. Part of Agrippa's massive water infrastructure program.

name: Aqua Virgo
description: Built to supply Agrippa's baths on the Campus Martius. Still flows today, feeding the Trevi Fountain (as the Acqua Vergine).

name: Aqua Alsietina
description: Built by Augustus primarily to fill the naumachia (artificial lake) for staged naval battles in Trastevere. Water was not potable.

name: Aqua Claudia
description: One of Rome's finest aqueducts, with impressive arcades still standing at Porta Maggiore. Praised by Frontinus for water quality.

name: Anio Novus
description: Highest-capacity of Rome's aqueducts, sharing arcades with the Aqua Claudia. Drew water from the upper Anio valley.

name: Aqua Traiana
description: Built by Trajan to supply the Trastevere district from springs near Lake Bracciano. Restored by Paul V as the Acqua Paola.

name: Aqua Alexandrina
description: Last of the eleven aqueducts of Rome, built to supply the Baths of Alexander Severus on the Campus Martius.

name: Pont du Gard / Aqueduct of Nemausus
description: Iconic three-tiered aqueduct bridge crossing the Gardon river. Among the best-preserved Roman structures. UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Aqueduct of Segovia
description: Remarkably preserved double-arched aqueduct with 167 arches built without mortar. Symbol of the city. UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Aqueduct of Carthage (Zaghouan)
description: One of the longest aqueducts in the Roman Empire, running 132 km from Zaghouan to Carthage. Restored by Hadrian after a drought.

name: Aqueduct of Valens
description: Part of Constantinople's elaborate water system, with a total length of 250 km including feeder channels. The double-arched bridge survives in modern Istanbul.

name: Caesarea High-Level Aqueduct
description: Double-channel aqueduct bringing water from springs on Mount Carmel to Herod's showpiece city. Expanded under Hadrian.

name: Caesarea Low-Level Aqueduct
description: Second aqueduct at Caesarea, tapping local springs to supplement the high-level channel as the city grew.

name: Aqueduct of the Gier
description: Longest and most impressive of Lyon's four aqueducts, with inverted siphons crossing deep valleys. Best-preserved siphon infrastructure in the Roman world.

name: Aqueduct of the Brévenne
description: Second of Lyon's four aqueducts, drawing water from the Brévenne valley northwest of the city.

name: Aqueduct of the Yzeron
description: Earliest of Lyon's four aqueducts, supplying water from the nearby Yzeron valley.

name: Aqueduct of Mont d'Or
description: Third of Lyon's four aqueducts, the shortest, drawing water from the nearby Mont d'Or springs.

name: Aqueduct of Les Ferreres (Tarragona)
description: Double-tiered aqueduct bridge known as the Pont del Diable (Devil's Bridge). Supplied the provincial capital of Hispania Tarraconensis.

name: Cornalvo Aqueduct
description: One of three aqueducts serving the Lusitanian capital. The Cornalvo dam and reservoir are still in use today.

name: Proserpina Aqueduct
description: Supplied by the Proserpina dam, one of the best-preserved Roman dams. Fed the monumental fountains and baths of Mérida.

name: Los Milagros Aqueduct
description: Impressive three-tiered aqueduct bridge crossing the Albarregas valley. Known as 'The Miracles' for its survival. 38 meters tall.

name: Aqueduct of Aspendos
description: Aqueduct with twin pressure towers using inverted siphon technology to cross the valley. Among the best-preserved siphon aqueducts.

name: Aqueduct of Pergamon (Madradag)
description: Hellenistic/Roman aqueduct with a remarkable pressurized lead pipe siphon reaching 190m differential, among the highest pressures in antiquity.

name: Aqua Classica (Ephesos)
description: One of several aqueducts serving Ephesos, supplying the fountains and baths of one of the largest cities in the Roman East.

name: Marnas Aqueduct (Ephesos)
description: Major aqueduct from the Marnas springs, with multiple arcaded sections. Expanded under successive emperors.

name: Aqueduct of Side
description: Long aqueduct with tunnels and bridges bringing water from the Taurus mountain springs to the Pamphylian coastal city.

name: Aqueducts of Antioch
description: Multiple aqueducts serving the great metropolis of Antioch, expanded by Caesar, Augustus, and later emperors.

name: Aqueduct of Apamea
description: Aqueduct supplying the important military and commercial city of Apamea on the Orontes.

name: Aqueduct of Gerasa
description: Aqueduct supplying the Decapolis city of Gerasa, feeding its elaborate nymphaeum and baths.

name: Aqueduct of Corinth (Hadrianic)
description: Long-distance aqueduct from Lake Stymphalos to Corinth, one of Hadrian's major building projects in Greece.

name: Hadrianic Aqueduct of Athens
description: Aqueduct built by Hadrian from the foothills of Parnitha to central Athens, with a large reservoir on Lycabettus hill.

name: Aqueduct of Nikopolis
description: Aqueduct serving Augustus's victory city of Nikopolis, founded to commemorate the Battle of Actium.

name: Aqueduct of Patara
description: Aqueduct with an inverted siphon supplying the Lycian capital and major port city of Patara.

name: Eifel Aqueduct (Cologne)
description: One of the longest aqueducts in the northern provinces, bringing water from the Eifel hills. Remarkable for its precise gradient engineering.

name: Aqueduct of Trier (Ruwer Valley)
description: Aqueduct from the Ruwer valley serving the imperial capital of the Western Empire, including the massive imperial baths.

name: Aqueduct of Caesarea (Cherchell)
description: Built by the client king Juba II for the Mauretanian capital, later expanded under direct Roman rule.

name: Aqueduct of Forum Julii
description: Aqueduct serving the important naval base of Forum Julii in Gallia Narbonensis, with well-preserved arcaded sections.

name: Aqueduct of Arles
description: Aqueduct serving the important Gallic colony of Arelate, drawing water from the Alpilles hills.

name: Aqueduct of Aquae Sextiae
description: Aqueduct serving the spa town of Aquae Sextiae in southern Gaul, supplementing the natural thermal springs.

name: Aqueduct of Sens
description: Aqueduct serving the civitas capital of the Senones in Gallia Lugdunensis.

name: Aqueduct of Metz (Jouy-aux-Arches)
description: Aqueduct with a surviving arcaded bridge at Jouy-aux-Arches, serving the capital of the Mediomatrici in Belgica.

name: Aqueduct of Lincoln
description: Aqueduct supplying the hilltop colony of Lindum from springs to the northeast, with pipeline sections along the Roaring Meg.

name: Aqueduct of Dorchester
description: Aqueduct bringing water from the River Frome to Durnovaria, one of the best-documented aqueducts in Roman Britain.

name: Aqueduct of Thermae Himeraeae
description: Aqueduct supplying the Sicilian spa town of Thermae Himeraeae, with well-preserved arcaded sections.

name: Ponte delle Torri (Spoletium)
description: Spectacular aqueduct-bridge with ten arches spanning 230 meters and 76 meters high, crossing the gorge below Spoleto.

name: Aqueduct of Pompeii (Serino)
description: Branch of the great Serino aqueduct (Aqua Augusta) that supplied Pompeii and many other Campanian cities from springs near Serino.

name: Aqua Augusta (Serino to Misenum)
description: Enormous aqueduct system (Aqua Augusta) supplying the entire Bay of Naples from Serino springs to the naval base at Misenum. Supplied up to nine cities.

name: Aqueduct of Aventicum
description: Aqueduct supplying the capital of the Helvetii in western Switzerland, with remains of the distribution castellum.

name: Aqueduct of Diocletian's Palace
description: Aqueduct built to supply Diocletian's retirement palace at Spalatum from the Jadro river springs.

name: Aqueduct of Mytilene
description: Aqueduct with impressive arcaded sections supplying the chief city of Lesbos.

name: Aqueduct of Gades
description: Long aqueduct bringing water from the Sierra de las Cabras to the island city of Gades, one of the longest in Hispania.

name: Aqueduct of Italica
description: Aqueduct serving the birthplace of Trajan and Hadrian. Expanded during Hadrian's massive building program for the city.

name: Aqueduct of Toletum
description: Aqueduct supplying the hilltop city of Toletum in central Hispania, with a hydraulic system to raise water to the heights.

name: Aqueduct of Aquae Sulis
description: System managing both natural thermal spring water and fresh water supply for the renowned spa complex of Aquae Sulis.

name: Aqueduct of Perge
description: Aqueduct feeding the elaborate water-channel running down the center of Perge's main colonnaded street.

name: Aqueduct of Laodicea ad Lycum
description: Stone-pipe aqueduct with distinctive travertine-encrusted pipes. The lukewarm water referenced in the Book of Revelation.

name: Aqueduct of Bononia
description: Aqueduct serving the colony of Bononia on the Via Aemilia, drawing water from Apennine springs.

name: Bordj Djedid Cisterns Aqueduct
description: Secondary aqueduct feeding the massive Bordj Djedid cisterns at Carthage, supplementing the Zaghouan supply.

name: La Malga Cisterns Aqueduct
description: Aqueduct branch supplying the La Malga cisterns, the largest Roman cistern complex, holding over 50 million liters.

name: Aqueduct of Leptis Magna
description: Aqueduct supplying Septimius Severus's hometown from the Wadi Caam. Feeding the Hadrianic baths and Severan forum.

name: Wadi Lebda Aqueduct (Leptis Magna)
description: Second aqueduct at Leptis Magna, built as part of Severus's massive expansion of his native city.

name: Aqueduct of Thugga
description: Aqueduct feeding the Licinian Baths and other public buildings of the well-preserved hilltop city of Thugga in Africa Proconsularis.

name: Aqueduct of Thamugadi
description: Aqueduct supplying Trajan's veteran colony at Timgad from mountain springs. Fed the city's fourteen public baths.

name: Aqueduct of Cuicul
description: Aqueduct serving the mountainous colony of Cuicul in Numidia, feeding its baths and fountains.

name: Aqueduct of Volubilis
description: Short aqueduct from the Fertassa spring to the westernmost major Roman city in Africa, supplying its olive oil district and baths.

name: Aqueduct of Lambaesis
description: Aqueduct supplying the headquarters of Legio III Augusta in Numidia. Built during Hadrian's visit to the African frontier.

name: Eastern Aqueduct of Caesarea Mauretaniae
description: Second aqueduct at Cherchell, built after the annexation of Mauretania. Drew water from eastern mountain springs.

name: Aqueduct of Hippo Regius
description: Aqueduct supplying the city of St. Augustine from Taurus mountain springs, feeding its baths and forum fountains.

name: Aqueduct of Sabratha
description: Aqueduct supplying one of the three great cities of Tripolitania, feeding its theatre, baths, and forum.

name: Aqueduct of Tipasa
description: Aqueduct serving the coastal colony of Tipasa in Mauretania Caesariensis with its well-preserved nymphaeum.

name: Aqueduct of Thysdrus
description: Aqueduct supplying the wealthy olive-oil city of Thysdrus, home to the third-largest amphitheatre in the empire.

name: Aqueduct of Sufetula
description: Aqueduct supplying the prosperous interior city of Sufetula in Byzacena from nearby springs.

name: Aqueduct of Bulla Regia
description: Aqueduct serving the city famous for its underground villas, built to escape the African heat.

name: Aqueduct of Mactaris
description: Aqueduct supplying the highland city of Mactaris in Africa Proconsularis with its large bath complex.

name: Aqueduct of Madauros
description: Aqueduct serving the birthplace of Apuleius and a major centre of education in Roman Africa.

name: Kaikos Aqueduct (Pergamon)
description: Second major aqueduct at Pergamon, built under Hadrian to supply the expanded Asklepion sanctuary and lower city.

name: Kenchreios Aqueduct (Ephesos)
description: Third aqueduct of Ephesos, feeding the city's elaborate fountain houses and Library of Celsus complex.

name: Sirince Aqueduct (Ephesos)
description: Fourth aqueduct supplying Ephesos, built under Trajan to meet the growing water demands of one of the empire's largest cities.

name: Aqueduct of Smyrna (Kara Bunar)
description: Major aqueduct supplying the great Ionian metropolis, with arcaded sections still visible. Part of Hadrian's rebuilding after the 178 CE earthquake.

name: Yesilirmak Aqueduct (Smyrna)
description: Second aqueduct at Smyrna, rebuilt after the devastating earthquake of 178 CE with imperial funding from Marcus Aurelius.

name: Aqueduct of Daphne (Antioch)
description: Aqueduct from the famous springs of Daphne to Antioch, rebuilt by Trajan to supply the baths and fountains of the eastern capital.

name: Harbiye Aqueduct (Antioch)
description: Later aqueduct at Antioch drawing from mountain springs south of the city, part of the ongoing water expansion for a city of 500,000.

name: Aqueduct of Attaleia
description: Aqueduct serving the Pamphylian port city of Attaleia, enhanced during Hadrian's visit to southern Asia Minor.

name: Aqueduct of Miletus
description: Aqueduct supplying the ancient Ionian city of Miletus and its famous Faustina Baths from nearby springs.

name: Aqueduct of Aphrodisias
description: Aqueduct serving the famous city of Aphrodisias in Caria, centre of marble sculpture and the imperial cult.

name: Aqueduct of Sardis
description: Aqueduct rebuilt by Tiberius after the devastating earthquake of 17 CE destroyed the Lydian capital. Part of massive imperial relief.

name: Aqueduct of Hierapolis
description: Aqueduct supplying the spa and necropolis city of Hierapolis. The travertine terraces formed partly from aqueduct overflow.

name: Aqueduct of Priene
description: Hellenistic-Roman aqueduct with terracotta pipes supplying the hillside city of Priene overlooking the Maeander plain.

name: Aqueduct of Sagalassos
description: Aqueduct serving the Pisidian mountain city of Sagalassos, feeding its well-preserved monumental fountain (nymphaeum).

name: Aqueduct of Francolí (Tarragona)
description: Second aqueduct at Tarraco drawing from the Francolí river, supplementing the earlier Pont del Diable supply.

name: Aqua Vetus (Córdoba)
description: Early aqueduct serving the Republican-era provincial capital of Hispania Ulterior from Sierra Morena springs.

name: Aqua Nova (Córdoba)
description: Augustan aqueduct expanding water supply to the Baetica capital, feeding the new imperial forum and baths.

name: Rabo de Buey Aqueduct (Mérida)
description: Underground conduit aqueduct from the Rabo de Buey springs, a less-known fourth water supply for the Lusitanian capital.

name: Aqueduct of Conimbriga
description: Aqueduct supplying the well-preserved Lusitanian city of Conimbriga, feeding its elaborate mosaic-decorated fountains.

name: Aqueduct of Baelo Claudia
description: Aqueduct supplying the garum fish-sauce factory town on the Straits of Gibraltar, with three separate conduits from mountain springs.

name: Aqueduct of Narbo Martius
description: Aqueduct serving the capital of Gallia Narbonensis from Montagne Noire springs, with well-preserved sections.

name: Aqueduct of Burdigala
description: Aqueduct serving the major Aquitanian port of Burdigala, later capital of the poet Ausonius.

name: Aqueduct of Limonum
description: Aqueduct supplying the Pictones capital in Aquitania, with traces of the conduit surviving near the modern city.

name: Aqueduct of Augustodunum
description: Aqueduct serving Augustus's showpiece city for the Aedui, designed to rival Rome in urban infrastructure.

name: Aqueduct of Mediolanum Santonum
description: Aqueduct serving the capital of the Santones, crossing the Charente valley with an impressive arcaded bridge.

name: Aqueduct of Durocortorum
description: Aqueduct serving the capital of Gallia Belgica and one of the largest cities in the northern provinces.

name: Aqua Julia Campana (Capua)
description: Branch of the Campanian aqueduct system supplying ancient Capua, one of the largest cities in pre-Social War Italy.

name: Aqueduct of Verona
description: Aqueduct supplying the important Transpadane colony of Verona from springs in the Lessini Mountains.

name: Aqueduct of Ravenna
description: Aqueduct built by Augustus for the Adriatic naval base at Ravenna, later capital of the Western Empire.

name: Aqueduct of Catina
description: Aqueduct drawing water from Etna's springs to the major Sicilian city of Catina, with sections carved through lava rock.

name: Aqueduct of Tarracina
description: Aqueduct supplying the Volscian coastal city on the Via Appia, with remains near the ancient forum.

name: Aqueduct of Ostia
description: Aqueduct serving Rome's harbour city of Ostia, built as the city expanded from a military colony to a major commercial port.

name: Aqueduct of Minturnae
description: Aqueduct from the Monti Aurunci supplying the colony of Minturnae on the Via Appia at the Garigliano river.

name: Peisistratid-Roman Aqueduct (Athens)
description: Ancient Athenian aqueduct from the Hymettus springs, originally Archaic-era, restored and expanded under Roman rule.

name: Aqueduct of Argos
description: Hadrianic aqueduct supplying the ancient Peloponnesian city of Argos and its Roman-era baths.

name: Aqueduct of Patras
description: Augustan aqueduct serving the Roman colony of Patrae in Achaea, refounded after the Battle of Actium.

name: Aqueduct of Salona
description: Aqueduct serving the capital of Dalmatia from the Jadro springs, later also supplying Diocletian's nearby palace.

name: Aqueduct of Thessalonica
description: Aqueduct supplying the major Macedonian city of Thessalonica, capital of the province, from Chortiatis mountain springs.

name: Aqueduct of Philippopolis
description: Aqueduct supplying the Thracian capital of Philippopolis from Rhodope mountain springs, feeding its stadium and baths.

name: Aqueduct of Serdica
description: Aqueduct supplying the Thracian city of Serdica, later favored by Constantine who called it 'my Rome.'

name: Aqueduct of Ratae Coritanorum
description: Aqueduct serving the civitas capital of the Coritani in Roman Britain, supplying its public baths (the Jewry Wall).

name: Aqueduct of Viroconium
description: Aqueduct from the Bell Brook serving the fourth-largest city in Roman Britain, feeding its massive bath-basilica complex.

name: Aqueduct of Deva Victrix
description: Aqueduct supplying the legionary fortress of Legio XX at Deva from springs east of the city along Boughton ridge.

name: Aqueduct of Londinium
description: Timber and lead pipe conduit supplying the provincial capital of Londinium, supplementing well water in the growing city.

name: Herresthal Aqueduct (Trier)
description: Second aqueduct at Trier from the Herresthal springs, built to meet growing demand from the expanding imperial residence.

name: Aqueduct of Mogontiacum
description: Aqueduct serving the major legionary fortress on the Rhine frontier, headquarters of the Upper Germanic army.

name: Aqueduct of Vindobona
description: Aqueduct supplying the Danubian legionary fortress from springs in the Vienna Woods, with traces surviving in modern Vienna.

name: Aqueduct of Aquincum
description: Aqueduct with well-preserved pillar remains on Szentendre island, serving the capital of Pannonia Inferior on the Danube.

name: Aqueduct of Carnuntum
description: Aqueduct serving the major Pannonian legionary base and later imperial residence where Marcus Aurelius wrote the Meditations.

name: Aqueduct of Palmyra
description: Aqueduct from Efqa spring supplemented by qanats, supplying Zenobia's desert caravan city with its monumental colonnaded street.

name: Northern Qanats (Palmyra)
description: Network of underground qanats supplementing the main aqueduct at Palmyra, tapping desert aquifers for the growing city.

name: Aqueduct Tunnel of Gadara
description: Remarkable 170 km aqueduct, the longest known tunnel aqueduct in antiquity, serving the Decapolis city of Gadara.

name: Birketein Aqueduct (Gerasa)
description: Second aqueduct at Gerasa from the Birketein reservoir, a monumental biapsidal pool feeding the city via gravity channels.

name: Hadrianic Extension (Caesarea Maritima)
description: Major extension of the Caesarea aqueduct system under Hadrian, doubling the high-level channel to a second parallel conduit.

name: Aqueduct of Bostra
description: Aqueduct serving the capital of the new province of Arabia Petraea after Trajan's annexation of the Nabataean kingdom.

name: Aqueduct of Scythopolis
description: Aqueduct feeding the elaborate fountain and bath complex of the largest Decapolis city west of the Jordan.

name: Aqueduct of Tyre
description: Aqueduct from the Ras el-Ain springs to the island city of Tyre, the Phoenician metropolis, crossing a causeway built by Alexander.

name: Aqueduct of Berytus
description: Aqueduct serving the Augustan colony and famous law school of Berytus from springs in the Lebanese mountains.

name: Aqueduct of Alexandria
description: Roman-era expansion of Alexandria's canal-fed water system, supplementing the city's famous underground cisterns.

name: Aqueduct of Cyrene
description: Roman aqueduct expanding the water supply of the ancient Greek colony of Cyrene in Libya, fed by karst springs.

name: Aqueduct of Pola
description: Aqueduct serving the Istrian colony of Pola with its well-preserved amphitheatre, drawing from inland springs.

name: Aqueduct of Nemausus (Second)
description: Second aqueduct at Nîmes from nearby springs, supplementing the famous Pont du Gard supply for the growing colony.

name: Aqueduct of Vienna Allobrogum
description: Aqueduct serving the major Gallic city of Vienne on the Rhône, rival of Lyon, from eastern hill springs.

name: Aqueduct of Complutum
description: Aqueduct supplying the central Hispanian city of Complutum from springs in the Henares valley.

name: Aqueduct of Augusta Praetoria
description: Aqueduct supplying the strategic Alpine colony controlling the Great and Little St. Bernard passes.

name: Aqueduct of Colonia Ulpia Traiana
description: Aqueduct serving Trajan's colony on the Lower Rhine, one of the major cities of Germania Inferior.

name: Aqueduct of Sitifis
description: Aqueduct serving the major highland colony of Sitifis in Mauretania Sitifensis, later a provincial capital.

name: Aqueduct of Cirta
description: Aqueduct supplying the clifftop Numidian capital of Cirta, rebuilt after Caesar's colonization of the city.

name: Aqueduct of Emesa
description: Aqueduct serving the Syrian city of Emesa, home of the sun god Elagabal and birthplace of the Severan dynasty.

name: Aqueduct of Calleva Atrebatum
description: Short aqueduct serving the tribal capital of the Atrebates in Roman Britain, with traces of timber-lined channels.

name: Aqueduct of Sparta
description: Hadrianic aqueduct bringing water to the Lacedaemonian city, part of the emperor's program of philhellenic building across Greece.
